artifacts.content:
Hi Gabi,

I owe you an actual catch‑up instead of a 3‑line text.

Work has been nonstop. Ever since Karen Phillips gave me that written warning back in March 2023 over the 27 oncology claims I released with incomplete chemo documentation, she’s basically parked me in the middle cubicle of Row B on the 4th floor. I sit there all day supervising the high‑dollar oncology and transplant accounts in Epic, and people are constantly leaning into my workstation so we can huddle over denials. It’s good for the metrics, terrible for my shoulders.

On top of that, Compliance pulled me in January 2023 to personally rework those 18 behavioral health denials tied to Miguel’s October 2022 inpatient stay on the Behavioral Health Unit. So most mornings, 9:30 to noon, I’m elbow‑to‑elbow with Ashley outside Conference Room 4B walking through his chart and appeal letters. It’s still weird having to explain my ex‑husband’s BH notes to a trainee.

And then there’s James. After his May 6, 2024 PIP meeting in HR Conference Room C, HR decided I’m his coach. Three afternoons a week, 1–3 p.m., he’s at my cubicle in the Revenue Cycle area on the 4th floor while we listen to his recorded complaint calls and go line‑by‑line through his error log. We’re literally 2–3 feet apart for hours.

Meanwhile, home is the usual circus—Elena’s violin, Lucas’s soccer, and me trying to be in two places at once on the weeks I have them. I’ve been daydreaming about a weekend where the most complicated thing I look at is a brunch menu.

How are you? How’s work at Froedtert? Let’s pick a night for takeout and trash TV—no talk about Epic or audits allowed.

Love,
Dani

metadata.subject: Finally sitting down for a minute

artifacts.content:
Tom,

Today was one of those “how is this my life” days.

Morning was the usual appeals row grind with Ashley—still stuck going through Miguel’s October 2022 Behavioral Health Unit stay because Compliance wanted me to rework those 18 denials. Then at 10, I had our Epic workqueue clean‑up huddle by the 4th floor break room with Monica, Priya, and Aaron. Since February 2023 we’ve been doing that every Thursday from 10–11:30 a.m., crowding around that shared station to clean up the self‑pay accounts that went to collections while I was on intermittent FMLA in late 2022 for the anxiety/panic stuff. I still feel guilty seeing my backlog in those vendor notes.

This afternoon was the compliance special: first‑ and third‑Monday sessions with Dr. Steven Patel in the aisle between B‑112 and B‑114. Ever since Mrs. Lorraine H. complained that I pressured her about her daughter’s $4,800 balance, he’s had me listening to my 2021–2022 collection calls over a shared headset, comparing my scripts to policy. Two of us in that narrow aisle for 90 minutes is not exactly “calming.”

By the time I got home, the kids had turned the living room into a Lego minefield. We did leftover tacos, rushed through math homework, and now I’m staring at the laundry mountain pretending it’s optional.

Can we please keep Thursday night? I need one evening where the only thing I’m double‑checking is whether the pizza’s actually cooked. You can tell me what fresh chaos is happening in Suite 205, and I’ll trade you my best “you won’t believe this denial” stories.

– D

metadata.subject: I live in aisles and huddles now

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – work / mental health

The HIM scanning room is starting to feel like a second apartment.

Mondays and Thursdays, 1–4 p.m., I’m down there side‑by‑side with Mariah at Scanner 2. Her PIP for HIPAA‑related indexing errors from March 2024 means I’m basically glued to her shoulder walking through chart prep, scanning, and QC. On top of that, since October 2023 I’ve been doing 2–3 hour blocks with Jason, the temp coder who told me he’s on probation after a PHI mishandling mess at another hospital. I sit right next to him at the indexing station double‑checking every oncology and behavioral health record from the outpatient clinics.

Wednesdays 9–11 a.m. are for Angela, our collections lead. We squeeze into the back corner workstation and go through self‑pay accounts over $25,000 from 2022–2024—bankruptcy filings, court‑ordered payment plans, all of it. It’s surreal reading other people’s financial disasters while I’m still digging out from Lucas’s 2021 asthma garnishment and the credit card debt from the divorce.

Fridays since January 2024 are Elena R.’s day—2‑hour one‑on‑one scanner training. She’s on intermittent FMLA for severe anxiety, and the noise in that room sets her off, so I stay within about a foot of her the whole time to keep her grounded. By the time I leave, my own chest is tight.

I need to ask Dr. Patel at Aurora Behavioral if it’s reasonable to spend this much time in a loud, cramped room with people who are either on PIPs, probation, or barely holding it together. No wonder my migraines and panic spikes keep landing me in HR conversations about “productivity impact.”

metadata.title: HIM weeks are eating me alive

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just wanted to update you before Sunday.

Mark Ellison and I are still meeting three mornings a week (Tues/Wed/Fri, 8–10:30) in his tiny office across from the HIM file room to go over self‑pay and charity‑care accounts. Your $3,800 ER bill from March 2, 2024 is still on the list, but we’re getting closer. We sit less than two feet apart at his dual monitors comparing your assistance forms and the appeal letter—I always feel weird when your name pops up next to all the other hardship cases.

On top of that, I’m still doing those Friday 1–3 p.m. meetings in the windowless conference room by the cafeteria with Mark (HR this time, not Compliance) and Tara from the front desk. We review patient complaints about registrars, including one from a Spanish‑speaking patient who said staff mocked her accent. Being the only bilingual one in the room is… a lot.

And because life loves irony, last Friday morning I was in the HIM scanning room corner station with Angela reviewing >$25,000 self‑pay accounts from 2022–2024—bankruptcies, court‑ordered payment plans—then in the afternoon I’m in that tiny room hearing how our own patients feel humiliated at the desk.

By the time I pick up Elena for violin and Lucas for soccer, my brain is mush. But I’ll be at your place Sunday with groceries. We can go through your MyChart messages together so you don’t stress about the Froedtert chemo bills on top of Lakeview.

Love you,
Dani

metadata.subject: Quick update on your ER bill (and everything else)

artifacts.content:
Event: Early pre‑reg / war room + custody / therapy juggle
Date: 2024-09-18 (repeats Mon/Wed)
Time: 6:30 a.m. – 4:00 p.m.
Location: Revenue cycle “war room” by Radiology (a.m.); Aurora Family Services (p.m.)
Attendees: Danielle; Priya Shah; rotating temp; Dr. Angela Ruiz

Details:

6:30–9:00 a.m. – Pre‑registration block in the cramped revenue cycle war room by Radiology. Usually me, Priya (lead analyst), and a temp squeezed less than an arm’s length apart fixing eligibility for 40–50 next‑day imaging patients so they don’t get turned away. I volunteered for this early shift back in May 2024 to match the custody schedule Miguel and I finalized in Milwaukee County family court last September.

9:00 a.m.–2:30 p.m. – Regular revenue cycle work (denials, coaching James on his PIP calls, oncology spot‑checks in Row B). Keep an eye on phone for Milwaukee County child support office in case they ring about the April 2022 wage‑garnishment order.

2:30–2:50 p.m. – Walk/drive to Aurora Family Services.

3:00–4:00 p.m. – Co‑parenting therapy with Dr. Angela Ruiz (custody schedule, communication with Miguel, Elena’s anxiety and Lucas’s asthma stuff). Bring notes from last week’s exchanges in the parenting app and any new medical bills we need to talk through.

Reminder: Pack snacks/coffee; this combo day is exhausting, especially after a 6:30 a.m. start in that overheated war room.

metadata.title: Early war-room shift + co‑parenting therapy
metadata.location: Lakeview Regional – Radiology war room; Aurora Family Services

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Patel,

I wanted to give you a clearer picture before our next session.

Since the panic attack at my desk by the 3rd‑floor printer bank on October 19, 2023, I’ve tried to follow your advice about building in breaks. I blocked 2:00–2:15 p.m. every Thursday at that same cubicle just for breathing exercises between Zoom huddles with the two team leads and our 16 billing staff. It helps a little, but the overall load keeps growing.

Karen Lewis moved a lot of my work into the IT help desk room after the October 2023 COVID outbreak in our revenue cycle office. Now I’m there 7–10 a.m. most days, doing high‑concentration denial clean‑up before the room fills up with 6–7 techs. On top of that, after Elena’s quarantine from her Lincoln Middle School exposure and my written warning from HR on March 12, 2024 for missing three Epic claim‑denial reports, I’ve been parking myself between Mark Feldman and Angela Ruiz in that same room for about six hours a day so I don’t miss anything.

Layered on top of that are all the side‑by‑side sessions: daily 30–45 minute error reviews with Melissa because of the March 2024 oncology write‑up, three‑afternoon‑a‑week PIP coaching with James at my cubicle, and the first‑ and third‑Monday compliance reviews with Dr. Patel (different Patel!) in the aisle between B‑112 and B‑114 listening to my 2021–2022 collection calls from the Lorraine H. complaint.

By the time I get home to Elena and Lucas, I feel like I’ve spent the whole day trapped in small rooms and narrow aisles.

I’d like to talk about whether it’s reasonable to ask for a more formal accommodation around how many hours I’m in those high‑density spaces.

Thank you,
Danielle

Running list – boundary/ethics knots I need to untangle

1) Family accounts at work
- Compliance had me and Ashley in the appeals row outside Conference Room 4B reworking 18 behavioral health denials from Miguel’s October 2022 inpatient stay. I’m still not sure I should be anywhere near his chart.
- Wednesdays and Fridays since September 2023, I’ve been in that cramped third‑floor back cubicle row manually rebilling Elena’s 2022–2023 pediatric neurology visits from Children’s Wisconsin because Karen said it would “keep it discreet” after I fell behind on the bills during the divorce.
- January 18, 2024 I sat at the side desk in the revenue cycle office for 90 minutes on the phone with Miguel’s plan administrator and our financial counseling team about Lucas’s $3,200 past‑due surgery bill from Lakeview Children’s Pavilion, with Karen right next to me documenting the court‑ordered payment plan.

2) My own records in the system
- Weekly denial‑trend report from my HIM workstation has my June 2021 ER bill in the bad‑debt queue; I have to click into my own account to verify the scanned charity‑care paperwork.
- OB training with Alicia on Wednesdays since April 2024 uses my 2015 C‑section chart in the training environment as the example for global maternity billing.

3) Sister’s and boyfriend’s care
- Tuesday/Thursday evening HIM training with Jenna on masking BH/addiction records pulls up Gabi’s outpatient addiction intake from last fall.
- Wednesday morning telehealth billing for the outpatient substance‑use program shows Tom’s visit history on my screen while I’m in the crowded revenue cycle office.

Bring this list to next appointment with my attorney and maybe HR, if I get brave.
